that is, there is no sense in which species are either "lindy" or "ageing". why?

text:
the notion of "lindy-ness" or "deterioration" is encoded in the shape parameter k of the Weibull distribution— how long does a population member survive, when the rate of failure is varying with time?
https://t.co/os35gJgwi3

raw.full_text:
the notion of "lindy-ness" or "deterioration" is encoded in the shape parameter k of the Weibull distribution— how long does a population member survive, when the rate of failure is varying with time?
https://t.co/os35gJgwi3

text:
k < 1 → decreasing failure rate; "lindy-ness"; a selection process has weeded out failures and survivors are stronger

k > 1 → increasing failure rate; "deterioration / ageing"; population members get worse and are more likely to fail over time

k = 1 → constant failure rate

raw.full_text:
k < 1 → decreasing failure rate; "lindy-ness"; a selection process has weeded out failures and survivors are stronger

k > 1 → increasing failure rate; "deterioration / ageing"; population members get worse and are more likely to fail over time

k = 1 → constant failure rate

when you're dealing with arbitrary data structures, this gets tricky. what if I use a big data structure in two places and make small changes to it?

I can't mutate it, or the other path will "see" the mutation

do I have to copy the entire data structure every time? that's slow https://t.co/610rzbFc7e

text:
the trick is to use immutable data structures

these do a lot of internal data sharing in very flat, wide trees, so that mutations can be very fast while sharing most of the structure with the un-mutated data. these can sometimes be more efficient than mutable data structures!

the trick is to use immutable data structures

these do a lot of internal data sharing in very flat, wide trees, so that mutations can be very fast while sharing most of the structure with the un-mutated data. these can sometimes be more efficient than mutable data structures!

text:
relaxed radix balanced trees allow you do every operation you want on a list in O(log(n)) time or better. 

because the branching factor on the internal tree is so high, that log n often acts more like a constant

example of a failure that feeds this intuition:

I have some code which implements a hash array mapped trie. basically an immutable dictionary implemented on a sparse hash-prefix tree

example of a failure that feeds this intuition:

text: I have an iterator over the tree which basically just gets the next item by traversing the tree from the root. this is slow. I asked claude to improve it by caching a recent tree node to avoid re-traversal

raw.full_text: I have an iterator over the tree which basically just gets the next item by traversing the tree from the root. this is slow. I asked claude to improve it by caching a recent tree node to avoid re-traversal

text:
I pointed out that because HAMTs are sparse, leaf nodes might have low occupancy, and so leaf-caching would yield weak results. instead it should try caching an internal node

it gets to work and comes up with something. mediocre results, we start to debug

raw.full_text:
I pointed out that because HAMTs are sparse, leaf nodes might have low occupancy, and so leaf-caching would yield weak results. instead it should try caching an internal node

it gets to work and comes up with something. mediocre results, we start to debug

text: turns out it had just totally failed to understand what was going on; it was basically just looking for items stored on the internal node itself rather than inside the subtree, and if it missed, it would reset the whole thing

raw.full_text: turns out it had just totally failed to understand what was going on; it was basically just looking for items stored on the internal node itself rather than inside the subtree, and if it missed, it would reset the whole thing

text: it just didn't seem to get that the whole tree being traverse in order means that a subtree can be traversed in order, and that this is the operation it needed to encapsulate

text:
it's not sufficient to align an agent to any of these to get a "good" future

- what you ask for might not get you what you want
- what you want might not make you happy
- what makes you happy might be bad for you / the world
- your values can change & are changed by what you get

@davidad it fact, it _must_ be the case such situations exist, because of computational substrate independence

when one causal system perfectly simulates another, the substrate is "erased". the simulated system cannot be affected by details below the level of the simulation

@cube_flipper @Lasermazer in an (approximately) continuous system, you can see this as "sensitivies" in the dynamical landscape

text: @cube_flipper @Lasermazer think about what that means in phase space, where the position and momentum of each particle is its own dimension. the current state of the system is a smear of probability somewhere inside that volume, in the region where the positions & momenta of the particles "actually are"

raw.full_text: @cube_flipper @Lasermazer think about what that means in phase space, where the position and momentum of each particle is its own dimension. the current state of the system is a smear of probability somewhere inside that volume, in the region where the positions & momenta of the particles "actually are"

text:
@cube_flipper @Lasermazer so what does it mean in phase space for the macroscopic state to be insensitive to small changes?

it means if you change one electron— you push the system along the axis for that electron's x or P— no other variables change much; the big smear doesn't change where it is

raw.full_text:
@cube_flipper @Lasermazer so what does it mean in phase space for the macroscopic state to be insensitive to small changes?

it means if you change one electron— you push the system along the axis for that electron's x or P— no other variables change much; the big smear doesn't change where it is

text:
@cube_flipper @Lasermazer the only way to change the state of the gate is to move a whole bunch of electrons at once, in a coordinated way. you have to make a change with a high degree of correlation between all the parts

in phase space, this means you move along some diagonal in high-dimensional space

raw.full_text:
@cube_flipper @Lasermazer the only way to change the state of the gate is to move a whole bunch of electrons at once, in a coordinated way. you have to make a change with a high degree of correlation between all the parts

in phase space, this means you move along some diagonal in high-dimensional space

text: @cube_flipper @Lasermazer that is to say, the big smear of system state can only move from one part of the landscape to another through narrow "channels" in the landscape, where many variables are moving together. if it moves in any other direction, it stays in the general vicinity of where it was before

raw.full_text: @cube_flipper @Lasermazer that is to say, the big smear of system state can only move from one part of the landscape to another through narrow "channels" in the landscape, where many variables are moving together. if it moves in any other direction, it stays in the general vicinity of where it was before

text: @cube_flipper @Lasermazer the shape of this landscape is the causal structure of the system. you could coarse grain this landscape by just describing where the "pipes" are, the directions in phase space that allow you to move from one macro state to the next

raw.full_text: @cube_flipper @Lasermazer the shape of this landscape is the causal structure of the system. you could coarse grain this landscape by just describing where the "pipes" are, the directions in phase space that allow you to move from one macro state to the next

text:
@cube_flipper @Lasermazer what this means is that if you capture all of the sensitivities-- the channels that state can move large distances through-- you can capture how the more detailed system will behave in its landscape even if you don't have every individual detail

that's coarse-graining
